edges.description: Terminal 22q13 deletions usually include SHANK3, producing the core synaptic scaffold lesion.
edges.source: Terminal 22q13.3 Deletion and Adjacent Gene Haploinsufficiency

edges.description: Adjacent genes within variably sized 22q13 deletions likely contribute to renal anomalies.
edges.source: Terminal 22q13.3 Deletion and Adjacent Gene Haploinsufficiency

edges.description: Lymphedema is an age-related feature observed in deletion cohorts.
edges.source: Terminal 22q13.3 Deletion and Adjacent Gene Haploinsufficiency

edges.description: SHANK3 loss disrupts postsynaptic scaffolding at excitatory synapses.
SHANK3 haploinsufficiency produces the core neurodevelopmental delay phenotype.
SHANK3-associated neurodevelopmental impairment severely affects expressive speech.
SHANK3 haploinsufficiency contributes to moderate-to-profound intellectual disability.
SHANK3-associated neurological dysfunction manifests early as neonatal hypotonia.
Structural excitatory synapse deficits reduce excitatory synaptic output.
SHANK3-deficient neural circuits may become vulnerable to later psychiatric decompensation.
edges.target: Neuropsychiatric decompensation in adolescence and adulthood

edges.description: Excitatory synaptic dysfunction contributes to autistic behavior in PMS.
SHANK3-deficient circuit imbalance increases seizure susceptibility in a subset of patients.
Adolescent/adult neuropsychiatric decompensation includes loss of previously acquired skills.
edges.source: Neuropsychiatric decompensation in adolescence and adulthood

nodes.description: Absent or severely delayed expressive language is a hallmark feature. In prospective evaluation, only 19% of patients used some words spontaneously to communicate.

evidence.reference_title: Prospective investigation of autism and genotype-phenotype correlations in 22q13 deletion syndrome and SHANK3 deficiency.
evidence.snippet: only five (19%) used some words spontaneously to communicate

evidence.reference_title: Molecular characterisation of the 22q13 deletion syndrome supports the role of haploinsufficiency of SHANK3/PROSAP2 in the major neurological symptoms.
evidence.snippet: all patients showed some degree of mental retardation and severe delay or absence of expressive speech, regardless of deletion size

nodes.description: A high proportion of individuals with PMS meet diagnostic criteria for autism spectrum disorder. In prospective evaluation with gold-standard instruments, 84% met criteria for ASD and 75% for autistic disorder.

evidence.reference_title: Prospective investigation of autism and genotype-phenotype correlations in 22q13 deletion syndrome and SHANK3 deficiency.
evidence.snippet: The sample was characterized by high rates of autism spectrum disorder: 27 (84%) met criteria for autism spectrum disorder and 24 (75%) for autistic disorder.

evidence.reference_title: The 22q13.3 Deletion Syndrome (Phelan-McDermid Syndrome).
evidence.snippet: more than 50% of patients show autism or autistic-like behavior, and therefore it can be classified as a syndromic form of autism spectrum disorders (ASD)

nodes.description: SHANK3 deficiency reduces postsynaptic scaffold organization at glutamatergic synapses, producing fewer structurally mature excitatory synapses and impaired glutamate receptor localization.

Loss of SHANK3 disrupts the balance between excitatory glutamatergic and inhibitory GABAergic signaling. iPSC-derived neurons from PMS patients show major defects in excitatory but not inhibitory synaptic transmission, with reduced AMPA- and NMDA-mediated currents and fewer excitatory synapses, while inhibitory transmission remains intact.

evidence.reference_title: SHANK3 and IGF1 restore synaptic deficits in neurons from 22q13 deletion syndrome patients.
evidence.snippet: We show that PMDS neurons have reduced SHANK3 expression and major defects in excitatory, but not inhibitory, synaptic transmission.

evidence.reference_title: Shank3 mutant mice display autistic-like behaviours and striatal dysfunction.
evidence.snippet: Cellular, electrophysiological and biochemical analyses uncovered defects at striatal synapses and cortico-striatal circuits in Shank3 mutant mice.

nodes.description: Most individuals with PMS show significant global developmental delay apparent in the first year of life, affecting motor, cognitive, and language milestones.

evidence.reference_title: The 22q13.3 Deletion Syndrome (Phelan-McDermid Syndrome).
evidence.snippet: this syndrome is characterized by neurological deficits which include global developmental delay, moderate to severe intellectual impairment, absent or severely delayed speech, and neonatal hypotonia

nodes.description: Moderate to profound intellectual disability is present in the majority of individuals with PMS. In prospective studies, 77% exhibited severe to profound intellectual disability.

evidence.reference_title: Prospective investigation of autism and genotype-phenotype correlations in 22q13 deletion syndrome and SHANK3 deficiency.
evidence.snippet: Most patients (77%) exhibited severe to profound intellectual disability

nodes.description: Peripheral lymphedema is an age-related feature of PMS that increases in prevalence with age, reported in approximately 8-15% of patients in cohort studies.

evidence.reference_title: Clinical and genomic evaluation of 201 patients with Phelan-McDermid syndrome.
evidence.snippet: the proportion reporting an autism spectrum disorder, seizures, and cellulitis, or presenting with lymphedema or abnormal reflexes increased with age

nodes.description: Hypotonia is frequently the first clinical sign, often noted at birth or in early infancy, and contributes to feeding difficulties and delayed motor milestones.

evidence.reference_title: The 22q13.3 Deletion Syndrome (Phelan-McDermid Syndrome).
evidence.snippet: this syndrome is characterized by neurological deficits which include global developmental delay, moderate to severe intellectual impairment, absent or severely delayed speech, and neonatal hypotonia

nodes.description: A subset of individuals with PMS experience significant neuropsychiatric deterioration beginning in adolescence or adulthood, with features of bipolar disorder, catatonia, psychosis, and progressive loss of previously acquired skills. Mean age of onset is approximately 20 years. The underlying mechanisms are not fully understood but may involve age-dependent vulnerability of SHANK3-deficient neural circuits.
nodes.id: Neuropsychiatric decompensation in adolescence and adulthood

evidence.reference_title: Neuropsychiatric decompensation in adolescents and adults with Phelan-McDermid syndrome: a systematic review of the literature.
evidence.snippet: Clinical presentations included features of bipolar disorder, catatonia, psychosis, and loss of skills, occurring at a mean age of 20 years.

nodes.description: Progressive loss of previously acquired motor, language, and cognitive skills occurs in a subset of PMS patients, typically in adolescence or adulthood. This can be accompanied by neuropsychiatric features including bipolar disorder and catatonia.

evidence.reference_title: Neuropsychiatric decompensation in adolescents and adults with Phelan-McDermid syndrome: a systematic review of the literature.
evidence.snippet: we conducted a systematic literature review and identified 56 PMS cases showing signs of behavioral and neurologic decompensation in adolescence or adulthood

evidence.reference_title: Delineation of the genetic and clinical spectrum of Phelan-McDermid syndrome caused by SHANK3 point mutations.
evidence.snippet: Other common findings were consistent with reports among individuals with 22q13.3 deletions, including hypotonia, motor skill deficits, regression, seizures, brain abnormalities, mild dysmorphic features, and feeding and gastrointestinal problems.

nodes.description: Structural renal anomalies occur in up to 40% of individuals with PMS, including renal cysts, hypoplasia, agenesis, hydronephrosis, vesicoureteral reflux, kidney dysplasia, horseshoe kidneys, and pyelectasis. Candidate genes beyond SHANK3 in the 22q13 region include UPK3A, FBLN1, WNT7B, and CELSR1.

evidence.reference_title: State of the Science for Kidney Disorders in Phelan-McDermid Syndrome: UPK3A, FBLN1, WNT7B, and CELSR1 as Candidate Genes.
evidence.snippet: The scientific literature suggests that up to 40% of individuals with PMS have kidney disorders, yet little research has been conducted on the renal system to assess candidate genes attributed to these disorders.

evidence.reference_title: Delineation of the genetic and clinical spectrum of Phelan-McDermid syndrome caused by SHANK3 point mutations.
evidence.snippet: renal abnormalities associated with 22q13.3 deletions do not appear to be related to the loss of SHANK3

nodes.description: SHANK3 is a master scaffolding protein at the postsynaptic density of glutamatergic synapses. It connects membrane-bound receptors including NMDA and AMPA receptors to the actin cytoskeleton. Loss of one copy of SHANK3 is the primary molecular lesion underlying core neurological features of Phelan-McDermid syndrome.

evidence.reference_title: The 22q13.3 Deletion Syndrome (Phelan-McDermid Syndrome).
evidence.snippet: Almost all of these deletions include the gene SHANK3 which encodes a scaffold protein in the postsynaptic densities of excitatory synapses, connecting membrane-bound receptors to the actin cytoskeleton.

evidence.reference_title: Molecular characterisation of the 22q13 deletion syndrome supports the role of haploinsufficiency of SHANK3/PROSAP2 in the major neurological symptoms.
evidence.snippet: Our analysis therefore supports haploinsufficiency of the gene SHANK3, which codes for a structural protein of the postsynaptic density, as a major causative factor in the neurological symptoms of 22q13 deletion syndrome.

evidence.reference_title: Disruption of the ProSAP2 gene in a t(12;22)(q24.1;q13.3) is associated with the 22q13.3 deletion syndrome.
evidence.snippet: ProSAP2 is a good candidate for this syndrome, because it is preferentially expressed in the cerebral cortex and the cerebellum and encodes a scaffold protein involved in the postsynaptic density of excitatory synapses.

nodes.description: Seizures occur in a significant proportion of PMS patients, with variable age of onset and seizure types. The proportion reporting seizures increases with age. Seizures are reported approximately three times more often in patients with maternal-origin deletions.

evidence.reference_title: Clinical and genomic evaluation of 201 patients with Phelan-McDermid syndrome.
evidence.snippet: the proportion reporting an autism spectrum disorder, seizures, and cellulitis, or presenting with lymphedema or abnormal reflexes increased with age

evidence.reference_title: Clinical and genomic evaluation of 201 patients with Phelan-McDermid syndrome.
evidence.snippet: Seizures were reported three times more often among patients with a de novo deletion of the maternal rather than paternal chromosome 22.

nodes.description: Most Phelan-McDermid syndrome cases are terminal 22q13.3 deletions that include SHANK3 and variably remove adjacent genes. This contiguous-deletion branch explains why some non-neurological features, especially renal anomalies, are enriched in deletion cases but not clearly attributable to SHANK3 point mutations alone.
nodes.id: Terminal 22q13.3 Deletion and Adjacent Gene Haploinsufficiency

evidence.reference_title: Clinical and genomic evaluation of 201 patients with Phelan-McDermid syndrome.
evidence.snippet: 22q13 genomic anomalies include terminal deletions of 22q13 (89 %), terminal deletions and interstitial duplications (9 %), and interstitial deletions (2 %).

evidence.reference_title: State of the Science for Kidney Disorders in Phelan-McDermid Syndrome: UPK3A, FBLN1, WNT7B, and CELSR1 as Candidate Genes.
evidence.snippet: The scientific literature suggests that up to 40% of individuals with PMS have kidney disorders, yet little research has been conducted on the renal system to assess candidate genes attributed to these disorders.
